questions.question: Explain the difference between HashMap and TreeMap with respect to ordering and performance. Also state which one to use when.
keyPoints: HashMap: O(1) average insert/lookup, unordered
TreeMap: O(log n) insert/lookup, maintains sorted keys
Use HashMap for fast lookup with no ordering needs
Use TreeMap when sorted iteration or range operations required

questions.question: What is collision handling in HashMap? Explain how Java 8 improved collision handling over earlier versions.
keyPoints: Collision: multiple keys map to same bucket
Java 7: LinkedList chaining, O(n) worst case
Java 8+: Treeify buckets > 8 elements, O(log n) worst case
Improves performance for hash-heavy inputs

questions.question: What are the three collection views of a Map? Explain with example code showing how to iterate over each.
keyPoints: keySet(): returns Set of all keys
values(): returns Collection of all values
entrySet(): returns Set of Map.Entry (key-value pairs)
All views reflect changes to original map

questions.question: Explain the load factor in HashMap. What happens when it is increased to 0.9 or decreased to 0.5?
keyPoints: Load factor determines when to resize
Default 0.75 balances space and collisions
Higher (0.9): more entries per bucket, more collisions, less memory
Lower (0.5): fewer collisions, more memory, better performance

questions.question: What is the difference between get(), getOrDefault(), and computeIfAbsent() methods in Map interface?
keyPoints: get(key): returns value or null if absent
getOrDefault(key, default): returns value or default without inserting
computeIfAbsent(key, function): computes and inserts if absent, returns computed/existing value
computeIfAbsent is atomic - useful for caching

questions.question: Why is Hashtable considered legacy while ConcurrentHashMap is recommended for concurrent access?
keyPoints: Hashtable: coarse-grained locking on entire map
ConcurrentHashMap: segment-based locking (Java 7) or CAS (Java 8+)
ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads/writes without full lock
Better throughput and scalability than synchronized Hashtable

questions.question: Write a method to count frequency of each character in a string using HashMap.
keyPoints: Iterate each character in string
Use getOrDefault or computeIfAbsent to increment count
Time complexity O(n) where n is string length
Handles all characters including spaces and special chars

questions.question: Implement an LRU (Least Recently Used) cache of fixed size using LinkedHashMap. Explain how the removeEldestEntry() method works.
keyPoints: Extend LinkedHashMap with accessOrder=true
Override removeEldestEntry(Map.Entry) to return eldest when size > capacity
Access order updates on get() and put() operations
Eldest entry evicted when new element added beyond capacity
Thread-safety note: wrap with Collections.synchronizedMap() or use ConcurrentHashMap

questions.question: Compare and contrast HashMap, LinkedHashMap, TreeMap, and Hashtable. Provide a decision guide for when to use each implementation.
keyPoints: HashMap: best general-purpose, O(1), allows null
LinkedHashMap: insertion/access order, O(1), allows null
TreeMap: sorted keys, O(log n), no null keys, range operations
Hashtable: legacy, synchronized, no nulls
Decision based on: ordering needs, performance requirements, thread safety, null handling

questions.question: Explain the internal working of HashMap in detail. Include discussion on hash function, bucket array, collision resolution, resizing, and treeification in Java 8+.
keyPoints: hashCode() → hash() method → index calculation using (n-1) & hash
Initial capacity 16, load factor 0.75 triggers resize to 32
Collision resolution:链表 (Java 7) → 红黑树 (Java 8+)
Treeify when bucket size > 8 and capacity >= 64
Resize doubles capacity, rehashes all entries
TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8, UNTREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 6, MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY = 64
